title: Math myths: streaks, expectations, and luck

choices: Believing past independent events change the odds of the next one (e.g. “due” for a win)
diagnosticQuestions.hint: A fair coin doesn’t owe you a heads after tails.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: You can win often and still lose money if:
choices: Losses are larger than wins — expected value matters, not win rate alone
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Small wins + rare huge losses = bad game.

teachSlides.title: Win rate isn’t enough
teachSlides.body: Expected value combines how often you win, how big wins are, and how big losses are. A 70% win rate can still lose if the 30% wipes you.

teachSlides.title: Random walk vs patterns
teachSlides.body: Markets have randomness. Some claim edges from patterns; others say much is noise. Honest traders measure results over many trades.

teachSlides.title: Humility
teachSlides.body: If you confuse luck for skill, you’ll size up at the wrong time. Track outcomes and stay skeptical of your own story.

recapSlide.title: Recap
recapSlide.body: No “due” trades, judge strategies by expected value, and admit randomness. Math literacy protects you from stories your brain loves.

masteryQuestions.prompt: After five losses in a row on a fair-odds game, the next outcome is:
choices: Still independent — past doesn’t change fair odds

masteryQuestions.prompt: Expected value in trading accounts for:
choices: Sizes and probabilities of wins and losses
masteryQuestions.explanation: It’s the long-run average outcome per trial.

choices: To separate luck from edge with enough data
Because brokers require it for tax refunds
To guarantee a win next week
masteryQuestions.explanation: Small samples lie; larger samples tell more truth.
